article_title: Princeton's T16 Pipeline Mines NASA's TESS Archive for 10,091 New Exoplanet Candidates, More Than Doubling the Mission's Tally
summary: Manual override: technical checks passed but a fabricated 90%-hot-Jupiters claim attributed to Universe Today must be removed or re-sourced. All other claims verified.

findings.message: Fabricated population statistic attributed to Universe Today
findings.details: The article states 'Universe Today reports that more than 90 percent of the candidates appear to be hot Jupiters, gas giants on multi-day orbits.' Universe Today does not contain this claim — its body discusses only the single confirmed gas giant around TIC 183374187, with no population breakdown. The arXiv paper itself contradicts the 90% figure, explicitly stating: 'our transit search also discovers other planetary types like warm Jupiters (giant planets with orbital periods 10 days<P<100 days), and Neptunes, even potential sub-Neptunes and super-Earths.' Either remove the claim or re-source it from a fact actually present in the cited material.

content_preview.title: Princeton's T16 Pipeline Mines NASA's TESS Archive for 10,091 New Exoplanet Candidates, More Than Doubling the Mission's Tally
content_preview.summary: A Princeton-led reanalysis of 83.7 million TESS light curves used random forest classifiers to surface 11,554 planet candidates, including one confirmed hot Jupiter, by reaching stars 16 times fainter than prior searches.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A team of astronomers at Princeton University has released the largest single-pass exoplanet candidate catalog ever drawn from NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS), surfacing 11,554 transit signals — 10,091 of them previously undetected — from a fresh reduction of the mission's first year of full-frame images. The work, posted to [arXiv on April 20, 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18579) and titled *The T16 Planet Hunt*, more than doubles TESS's previous candidate co...

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (786 words) with strong technical sourcing on most claims. One paragraph contains a fabricated population statistic that misrepresents the paper's actual findings.

source_verification.https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18579: source-0.html (abstract page) verified for: 83,717,159 light curves, 11,554 candidates, 10,091 new, 411 single-transit, 1,052 previously known, Magellan/PFS, TIC 183374187, metal-poor thick-disk, hot Jupiter, April 20 2026 citation date, authors Roth/Hartman/Bakos, orbital periods 0.5-27 days. Verified via WebFetch on arXiv HTML rendering for the random-forest classifier choice, the T=14.5 bright/faint split, 0.56±0.19 M_Jup mass, and 5.05907±0.00059-day orbital period — all confirmed in the paper body.
source_verification.https://www.universetoday.com/articles/t: source-1.html verified for: 11,554 candidates, TIC 183374187 single confirmation, 6.5m Magellan in Chile (Las Campanas Observatory in image caption), 'sixteen times fainter than those typically targeted by official TESS searches', single confirmed gas giant 'roughly the mass of Jupiter', orbit 'in just a few days'. CRITICAL: Universe Today does NOT contain any '90 percent' claim or any population-level breakdown of candidate planet types.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified except one: 'Universe Today reports that more than 90 percent of the candidates appear to be hot Jupiters' is fabricated — it appears in neither Universe Today nor the arXiv paper, and the arXiv paper directly contradicts it by describing the candidate set as including warm Jupiters, Neptunes, sub-Neptunes and super-Earths.
editor_notes.originality: Not previously covered in the archive. Internal cross-references to TOI-5205b JWST coverage and Roman Space Telescope unveiling are appropriate.
concerns: Fabricated population statistic attributed to Universe Today (single sentence, blocking publication).
recommendations: Remove the sentence beginning 'Universe Today reports that more than 90 percent of the candidates appear to be hot Jupiters' and replace it with a fact actually present in one of the cited sources. Suggested replacement: paraphrase the arXiv paper's own description of the candidate diversity (warm Jupiters with 10–100-day periods, Neptunes, sub-Neptunes, super-Earths), attributed to arXiv. All other technical claims verified — no further changes required.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: REQUEST_CHANGES. One surgical fix needed: remove or re-source the fabricated 90%-hot-Jupiters paragraph. Everything else (random forest classifiers, T=14.5 split, mass and period of TIC 183374187, candidate counts, author list, dates) is verified verbatim against the arXiv paper and the Universe Today snapshot.
